data.title: Trump's Affordability Agenda May Not Survive a New Iran Crisis — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Matt Peterson

With Americans stewing over the high cost of living, President Donald Trump is considering involving the U.S. in another conflict overseas. What he does about protests in Iran will matter for Americans' energy bills and could worsen inflation just as it is finally steadying.

Trump on Tuesday stopped just short of calling for Iranians to overthrow their government. "Iranian Patriots, KEEP PROTESTING — TAKE OVER YOUR INSTITUTIONS!!!" the president posted on social media. "HELP IS ON THE WAY."

The White House declined to clarify what "help" might consist of. A spokeswoman for the president referred a request for comment back to the president's post. The U.S. military, which is stationed in the region, attacked Iranian nuclear facilities at Trump's direction in June.

Oil prices jumped after Trump's post. Brent crude rose nearly $2 to $65.80 a barrel Tuesday morning, the highest level since November.

The Trump administration wants oil prices near $50 a barrel to help keep down prices at the pump.

Trump is due to speak on his affordability agenda in Detroit on Tuesday, but is also expected to meet with his national security team today to discuss potential policies toward Iran. It wasn't clear when the meeting would take place.

A deeper entanglement with Iran could swamp the effects of his efforts to lower housing prices and other costs that have frustrated Americans, especially because the country's dependence on energy exports offers a point where the U.S. could find leverage.

Iran is already under intense sanctions from the U.S. and others. Its currency has fallen 56% against the dollar since the nuclear strikes, according to data from Bonbast, a site that tracks the informal exchange rate.

A mass protest movement has sprung up in Iran, prompted by the economic woe driven by the currency crisis. The theocratic government has responded by shutting off communications with the outside world and launching a campaign of repression. Outside human-rights groups estimate hundreds or thousands of Iranians have been killed, though precise numbers are difficult to come by.

Iran exports just under two million barrels of crude oil a day. Like Venezuela, Iran relies on a so-called shadow fleet of illicit tankers that send oil to buyers such as China and India that don't comply with the U.S. sanctions regime.

Iran's leaders are nervously watching the fallout from the U.S. intervention in Venezuela. The U.S. seized President Nicolás Maduro on Jan. 3 and flew him to the U.S. for prosecution, but the bigger concern for Iran may be the continuing blockade of Venezuelan oil exports.

"Iran's economic situation — the ability of the U.S. to choke it — is the single biggest threat to the regime, more than military strikes," said Vali Nasr, senior advisor to the CSIS Middle East Program, at a briefing about the ripples from the U.S. moves in Venezuela.

Trump on Monday said the U.S. would impose 25% tariffs on any country doing business with Iran. The White House didn't clarify whether that policy would add to existing tariffs or would include China. Beijing is a major buyer of Iranian oil, but Trump has said before that it would be exempt from similar policies, and the U.S. and China have called a truce in their trade fight.

The tariffs could be a way for Trump to take action without actually bombing Iran again, wrote Rachel Ziemba, founder of the consulting firm Ziemba Insights, in her newsletter. "Overall for now the biggest impact could be reducing the near-term risk of military attacks, which is not a bad thing."

But if the U.S. does get involved in the Iranian protests, beyond Trump's call for protests, Americans could feel it. Directly cutting off Iranian oil exports by seizing tankers, like the U.S. is doing with Venezuela, could put pressure on oil prices.

Equally important, Iran has long threatened to attack or blockade oil shipments from the Persian Gulf as they pass through the Strait of Hormuz to the Indian Ocean. Military analysts think it has the ability to follow through, if it feels sufficiently threatened.

So far, the oil market has been well enough supplied that geopolitical disruptions have only briefly boosted prices. Inflation data Tuesday showed prices rising at a steady 2.7% in December.

Deeper entanglement in Iran could put that at risk. Iran didn't hit back after the U.S. took out its nuclear facilities. But the pressure on the regime has only grown since. If it believes Trump isn't just bluffing and is really taking action to force the ayatollahs out, it could lash out.

  • Reshma Kapadia contributed reporting to this article

Write to Matt Peterson at matt.peterson@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Weinstein's Saba Capital pushes for Workspace wind-down amid trading struggles
data.description:
Boaz Weinstein's Saba Capital, which holds about a 13.5% stake in London-focused flexible office-space provider Workspace , sent a letter on Tuesday urging the company to pursue a managed wind-down.

"Considering the company's (Workspace) persistent trading discount, refinancing challenges, and structural impediments in its shareholder base, the current model no longer represents the best path to value creation," U.S. activist investor Saba said in the letter.

Saba also accused Workspace of having a highly concentrated shareholder register, with a small number of holders controlling a "disproportionately large portion" of the shares.

The London-listed company's shares had fallen 7.6% in the last 12 months as of Tuesday's market close.

Workspace did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for a comment.

The British firm reported a pretax loss of 71.1 million pounds ($95.46 million) for the six months to September 30 as budget uncertainty led to delays in leasing decisions by small- and medium-sized businesses.

($1 = 0.7448 pounds)

data.title: Food Pain Points Hit Trump's Affordability Push — WSJ
data.description:
By Patrick Thomas

Rising beef and coffee and prices continue to inflate grocery bills and hurt President Trump's push to make food more affordable.

Retail ground beef prices are up nearly 16%, while steaks are up 15% between January and December 2025, according to Labor Department data.

Last fall, Trump made lowering beef prices a priority and tasked key advisers with making ribeyes and filets more affordable. Beef prices have been rising for years after ranchers began shrinking their cattle herds following a prolonged period of weakening financial conditions.

Coffee prices are up 20%, according to government data. Prices for the beverage have been accelerating since Trump's tariffs on Brazil, a major coffee exporter. Despite reducing tariffs on Brazil late last year, lower coffee costs have yet to work their way through the U.S. supply chain, according to analysts.

Egg prices have come off their record highs reached last year. Prices over the January-December period are down more than 31%, primarily due to a decline in bird flu cases in egg-laying flocks.

Bird flu cases have continued, but primarily in turkey flocks over the past two months, allowing egg producers to rebuild their decimated flocks. Still, prices remain well above historical levels.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Scott Adams, 'Dilbert' comic strip creator and Trump fan, dead at 68
data.description:
"Dilbert" comic strip creator Scott Adams, a vocal supporter of President Donald Trump whose career flagged after a racist rant, died on Tuesday, his former wife said. He was 68.

Shelly Miles announced Adams' passing in an online livestream in which she read a final message from the artist, whose strip lampooned life in the cubicle farms of corporate America, framed around its titular character, an engineer known for his glasses and perennially bent tie.

Adams first announced he had metastatic prostate cancer in May 2025 in his "Coffee with Scott Adams" video show and said he only had a few months to live.

He continued to document his decline on social media and made a direct appeal to Trump to get his healthcare provider, Kaiser Permanente of Northern California, to schedule treatment with the targeted radiotherapy drug Pluvicto.

"On it," Trump responded in a November 2 social media post. A day later Adams wrote on social media that he would begin receiving Pluvicto the next day.

On Tuesday, the Republican president noted the cartoonist's passing on Truth Social.

"Sadly, the Great Influencer, Scott Adams, has passed away. He was a fantastic guy, who liked and respected me when it wasn’t fashionable to do so. He bravely fought a long battle against a terrible disease," Trump wrote.

The "Dilbert" comic strip was first published in 1989 and ran for decades. At its peak, it was one of the most widely circulated comic strips in the U.S., but many newspapers dropped it in 2023 after a racist rant by Adams appeared on YouTube.

Billionaire Elon Musk defended Adams and accused the media of having a bias against whites and Asians.

Adams called Black Americans a "hate group" and suggested white Americans "get the hell away from Black people," in response to a conservative organization's poll purporting to show that many African-Americans do not think it is OK to be white.

He later said that his comments were intended as hyperbole and that he disavowed racists, and said that media reports had ignored the context of his comments.

data.title: US natgas output to hit record high in 2026, while demand declines, EIA says
data.description:
By Scott DiSavino

U.S. natural gas output will rise to a record high in 2026, while demand will decline, the U.S. Energy Information Administration said in its Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) on Tuesday.

EIA projected dry gas production will rise from a record 107.4 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) in 2025 to 108.8 bcfd in 2026 and 109.7 bcfd in 2027.

The agency also projected domestic gas consumption will decline from a record 91.5 bcfd in 2025 to 90.3 bcfd in 2026 and 90.9 bcfd in 2027.

data.title: Denmark's Rockwool says Russia has seized four of its factories
data.description:
Danish building materials company Rockwool said on Tuesday that Russia has taken control of the company's four factories in Russia and the company is no longer in control of its assets in the country.

Rockwool's statement said the factories will be de-consolidated and the net value of its businesses there will be written down, adding that their total equity amounted to 469 million euros ($546 million) at December 31.

The company added that it will defend its legal rights under the bilateral investment treaty between the countries, but added: "We are not optimistic about reversing the decision to place our Russian subsidiaries under forced external administration."

Rockwool shares fell 8% earlier on Tuesday after the seizure of two subsidiaries was reported by Bloomberg.

($1 = 0.8589 euros)

data.title: Nymex Overview: Oil Futures Rise as Geopolitical Tensions Increase Worries over Supply — OPIS
data.description:
Oil futures were trading at seven-week high early Tuesday afternoon on continued geopolitical concerns that traders worry could led to cuts in output.

The NYMEX February West Texas Intermediate contract was up $1.50 to $61.50/bbl at about 12:40 p.m. ET and the March WTI contract was $1.42 higher at $60.74/bbl.

The ICE Brent March contract was up $1.43 to $65.30/bbl and the April contract was $1.37 higher at $64.59/bbl.

Refined products were also posting solid gains. The NYMEX February RBOB contract was 3.03cts higher at $1.8241/gal and the March contract was up 3.08cts to $1.8528/gal. The February ULSD contract was up 7.55cts to $2.2299/gal and March was 6.92cts higher at $2.2175/gal.

The fallout from Iran's antigovernment demonstrations, including government crackdowns and escalating unrest, has heightened concerns over potential disruptions to production in the country. President Trump's public encouragement of protesters and his threat to impose 25% tariffs on countries trading with Iran have reinforced market risk sentiment, particularly given China's role as the main importer of Iranian crude.

U.S. intervention in Venezuela has opened the door for more oil from that country to reach the market. But questions about Iranian production and potential disruptions of exports from Kazakhstan have raised supply worries.

Some technical factors, including the unwinding of short positions, have also supported price momentum.

A series of disruptions at the Caspian Pipeline Consortium export terminal on Russia's Black Sea coast, including recent drone strikes and earlier weather and maintenance issues, has significantly constrained Kazakh export volumes.

Because the consortium accounts for the bulk of Kazakhstan's crude exports, the bottleneck is limiting flows and adding to supply concerns.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

By Allegra Fradkin, afradkin@opisnet.com; Editing by Jeffrey Barber, jbarber@opisnet.com

data.title: Measles cases in South Carolina rise by 124 to 434, state health department says
data.description:
The South Carolina health department reported 434 measles cases related to the ongoing outbreak in the state on Tuesday, 124 additional cases since its last update on Friday.

The widening outbreak has been reported in the northwest part of the state, which includes Greenville and Spartanburg, according to the South Carolina Department of Public Health.

Of those infected, 378 were unvaccinated, three were partially vaccinated with one of the recommended two-dose measles-mumps-rubella vaccines, six was fully vaccinated and 47 had unknown vaccination status.

Most cases were reported in children in the five to 17 age group followed by those below five years of age.

data.title: FTC Release: FTC Issues Biennial Report to Congress on the National Do Not Call Registry
data.description:
In FY 2025, the agency received more than 2.6 million complaints and 4.8 million new numbers were added to the DNC Registry

The Federal Trade Commission issued its biennial report to Congress on the National Do Not Call (DNC) Registry that shows consumers placed more than 258 million telephone numbers on the Registry as of the end of fiscal year 2025, an increase of more than 4.8 million from the previous fiscal year.

View Press Release: https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2026/01/ftc-issues-biennial-report-congress-national-do-not-call-registry

data.title: Even Oil Companies Are Nervous About Trump's Crackdown on Offshore Wind — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Avi Salzman

President Donald Trump has made no secret of his disdain for wind power. He even brought it up at a meeting with oil executives on Friday that was ostensibly about drilling in Venezuela.

"By the way, in case you people don't know, I'm not much of a windmill person," Trump said. "My goal is to not let any windmill be built."

His administration is making good on that promise, forcing work to stop on all offshore wind projects on Dec. 22 because of what officials said was a national security concern.

The Department of the Interior said that the turbines may block radar signals, based on classified reports. On Monday, a judge lifted the stop work order for one project off Rhode Island, but the other four that were under construction remain stalled, losing millions of dollars each day. Each project had already gone through a government permitting process lasting more than five years, which included reviews by defense and security officials.

On its face, Trump's blockage of wind projects looks like a good thing for fossil fuel producers. Wind power competes against resources like coal and natural gas, all of which are used to generate electricity. But oil companies also rely on predictable permitting decisions. And some have already raised concerns that their fossil fuel investments could be derailed by a change in presidential administrations.

"I certainly would like to see those projects that have been permitted in the past continue to be developed," Colette Hirstius, president of Shell USA, told the Financial Times last year. Shell previously walked away from its U.S. offshore wind investments, but sees the permitting uncertainty as a threat to its oil projects too, including in the Gulf of Mexico. "That type of permitting uncertainty has been utilized to undermine the permits that we have in the past," she said.

The American Petroleum Institute, or API, which advocates for the oil and gas industry, has made permitting reform its number one goal this year. "It takes far too long to build the infrastructure required to move energy where it's needed," API President Mike Sommers told reporters. "Red tape, delay, endless litigation, all drive up costs, limit supply, and strain reliability."

But permitting reform is now stalled because of offshore wind. Congress appeared to be closer than ever to passing a bipartisan bill that could speed the permitting process both for fossil fuel projects and renewables.

The bill was also expected to include provisions making it harder for administrations to cancel permits that had already been issued. That wouldn't just help offshore wind — it could also have stopped President Joe Biden from canceling the Keystone XL pipeline project for natural gas during his administration.

But Democrats walked away from the bill last month because of the Trump administration's moratorium on wind projects. They do not want to move forward if the administration is going to unilaterally cancel projects it doesn't like.

Asked by a Barron's reporter on Tuesday about whether API would back offshore wind in order to get a permitting deal, Sommers said his group is working with trade groups for alternative energy.

"It's time for both sides to put their weapons down," he said. "We're going to need... a lot more energy going forward, and that means all sources of energy. We are not in competition with each other."

The oil and gas industry will never fully embrace renewable energy. But to grow faster, it may need to quietly start advocating for those much-maligned windmills.

Laura Sanicola contributed reporting.

Write to Avi Salzman at avi.salzman@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: European Stocks Close Mixed After Cautious Session
data.description:
BRUSSELS/FRANKFURT/PARIS (dpa-AFX) - European stocks closed mixed on Tuesday as concerns about geopolitical tensions and U.S. President Donald Trump's new tariff threat rendered the mood cautious, forcing traders to refrain from making big moves. Some corporate updates provided a bit of direction.

The latest inflation data from the U.S. came in line with expectations, but raised expectations of more monetary easing in the coming months.

The pan European Stoxx 600 edged down marginally. The U.K.'s FTSE 100 slipped 0.03%, Germany's DAX edged up 0.06%, and France's CAC 40 closed down by 0.14%. Switzerland's SMI ended down by 0.46%.

Among other markets in Europe, Belgium, Denmark, Netherlands, Norway, Portugal and Turkiye closed higher.

Greece, Iceland, Ireland, Poland and Russia ended weak, while Czech Republic, Finland, Spain and Sweden closed flat.

In the UK market, Whitbread surged more than 7% as pressure on margins eased after the company said the impact of the UK budget on costs would be lower than previously expected.

Pershing Square Holdings climbed 2.7%. BP, Shell, Melrose Industries and British American Foods gained 2 to 2.7%.

JD Sports Fashion, Rolls-Royce Holdings, Rio Tinto, Fresnillo, Endeavour Mining, Beazley and Polar Capital Technology Trust also ended notably higher.

Smith & Nephew, Kingfisher, Convatec Group, Entain, Rentokil Initial, Vodafone Group, Admiral Group, Severn Trent, Persimmon, ICG, National Grid and 3i Group lost 2 to 4%.

In the German market, Symrise moved up more than 5%. Symrise AG shares surged nearly 7% after the company announced that it is in advanced discussions with potential buyers regarding the divestment of its terpenes business.

Zalando gained about 4.75% after Barclays upgraded the stock from equalweight to overweight and raised its price target to EUR 35.00, an increas of EUR 7.00.

Infineon and MTU Aero Engines both gained about 2%. Siemens gained nearly 1.5%, while Siemens Energy, Fresenius, Rheinmetall, Brenntag, Daimler Truck Holding and Commerzbank also closed on positive note.

Fresenius Medical Care, Heidelberg Materials, Continental, Deutsche Telekom, BMW and Deutsche Boerse lost 2 to 3%.

Hannover Rueck, Bayer, Merck, E.ON, Adidas, Qiagen and Munich RE also ended notably lower.

In the French market, TotalEnergies, Safran, Airbus, Capgemini, Eurofins Scientific, Pernod Ricard and STMicroElectronics gained 1 to 2.3%.

Vinci, TP, Saint Gobain, Stellantis, Veolia Environment, Carrefour, Edenred, Sanofi and Thales lost 2 to 4.3%.

AXA, Kering, Engie, Bouygues, Unibail Rodamco, Dassault Systemes and Publicis Groupe also closed weak.

In economic news, UK retail sales grew at a slower pace in December despite festive season as consumers cut back spending on higher household bills, the British Retail Consortium said Tuesday.

Total retail sales increased 1.2% in December from the previous year compared to an annual growth of 3.2% in the same period last year.

Food sales rose 3.1% on a yearly basis, while non-food sales decreased 0.3%. In-store non-food sales dropped 0.5% and online non-food sales fell 0.1%.

The online penetration rate increased to 38.6% in December from 38.5% in the last year.

Data showed France's central government budget deficit narrowed to EUR 155.4 billion at the end of November 2025, from EUR 172.5 billion in the same month a year earlier.

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Roblox Expected to Give Cautious Forecast for 2026 — Market Talk
data.description: Roblox will likely provide a cautious outlook for 2026, Raymond James analysts say in a note. Investors have concerns about Roblox's prior projection for margins to decline from infrastructure and safety investments, and they worry about whether the company will have more viral hits as it laps the rise of popular games from last year, the analysts say. "We believe the company is incentivized to be cautious," they say, noting even a 20% year-over-year booking growth rate could disappoint investors' expectations. "While we believe a lot of this concern is being reflected in the stock price given its recent tumble, we see this quarter as a potential soft spot as investors come to terms with the 2026 outlook," they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)
